content.overview:
Lassa fever is an acute viral haemorrhagic fever caused by Lassa virus (an arenavirus), endemic in West Africa (Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Liberia, Guinea). The natural reservoir is the Mastomys (multimammate) rat. WHO estimates 100,000–300,000 infections and ~5,000 deaths annually in West Africa. Not endemic in India, but global health security frameworks require preparedness for imported cases.

--- IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE ---
This information is for health awareness only. Lassa fever is not endemic in India but travel-related importation risk exists. Declare travel history to healthcare providers. Consult a qualified healthcare provider for any suspected exposure.
types: Severe Lassa fever with haemorrhagic and neurological complications (~20% of cases)
symptoms: Gradual onset fever, fatigue, malaise, headache
Sore throat, cough, nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, abdominal pain
Chest and back pain, facial oedema
Bleeding from gums, nose, eyes (severe cases)
Hearing loss in 25% of survivors (often permanent)
warning_signs: Bleeding from any mucosal surface with fever
Fever after travel to West Africa within last 21 days — report immediately
risk_factors: Contact with food/items contaminated by rat urine/faeces
Person-to-person via blood/body fluids (healthcare settings — major risk)
Travel to/residence in endemic West African countries
prevention: Store food in rodent-proof containers; rodent control
Strict infection prevention and control (PPE) in healthcare settings for suspected cases
Travellers: avoid contact with rodents in endemic areas
environmental_control: Community sanitation to reduce Mastomys rat populations
Proper waste disposal away from living areas
Rodent-proofing of homes (seal gaps, store grains safely)
vaccination: No licensed vaccine currently available; candidates in clinical trials
diagnosis: RT-PCR (blood/urine) — reference labs only, BSL-4 precautions
All suspect samples handled as Category A bioterrorism agents
treatment_awareness_only: Ribavirin (antiviral) — most effective if started within 6 days of illness; hospital/IV use only
Supportive care: IV fluids, dialysis if renal failure, management of bleeding
Treatment details are for awareness only. Consult a qualified healthcare professional.
when_to_see_a_doctor: Fever with bleeding after travel to West Africa within 21 days — seek immediate care, declare travel history
Healthcare workers exposed to suspected case — seek prophylaxis advice
india_specific_context: Not endemic in India; NCDC (ncdc.gov.in) and IDSP handle imported VHF cases
International Health Regulations (IHR 2005) require reporting of suspect imported cases
Report to NCDC Emergency Operations Centre: 011-23978046

faqs.question: Has Lassa fever occurred in India?
faqs.answer: Lassa fever is endemic to West Africa (Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Guinea, Liberia). There are no known cases indigenous to India. However, it can be imported through travellers from endemic areas, and India has airport surveillance for viral haemorrhagic fevers.

faqs.question: How does Lassa fever spread?
faqs.answer: Lassa virus is carried by the Mastomys rat (multimammate rat). Humans are infected through contact with rat urine, faeces, or through eating contaminated food. Person-to-person spread is possible through direct contact with blood or body fluids of infected individuals.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of Lassa fever?
faqs.answer: Fever, weakness, headache, sore throat, followed by nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea, chest pain, and in severe cases haemorrhage. 80% of infections are mild. Severe disease causes hearing loss (even after recovery in 25% of cases), shock, and multi-organ failure.

faqs.question: Is Lassa fever treatable?
faqs.answer: Antiviral drug ribavirin is effective, especially when given early. Supportive care is critical. Healthcare workers must use full personal protective equipment. Report any suspected case of viral haemorrhagic fever in a traveller from West Africa to the nearest government hospital and district health authority immediately.
